text: Today I'll be showing my technique for a robotic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. This is a very common operation that I do in my practice.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures 100 centimeters of the small bowel to designate the biliopancreatic limb.
The surgeon divides the omentum to facilitate the Roux limb's tension-free reach to the stomach. Subsequently, the small bowel is divided, separating the biliopancreatic limb from the Roux limb.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the small bowel mesentery, ensuring adequate length and mobilization of the roux limb.
The surgeon measures and runs another hundred centimeters of small bowel, designating it as the roux limb intended to connect to the stomach.

The surgeon begins creating an anastomosis between the biliopancreatic limb and the roux limb to form the common channel. The surgeon checks to ensure the roux limb is not twisted and that the mesentery is properly splayed open.

The surgeon uses scissors to create enterotomies in both the biliopancreatic limb and the roux limb, noting minor bleeding. A stapler is then introduced to perform the common jejunojejunostomy anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the newly formed common channel using a running absorbable V-lock suture in two layers, ensuring secure closure of the anastomosis.

The surgeon ensures the corners of the anastomosis closure are securely sutured to prevent leaks, running the suture forward to the superior corner before beginning lumbar suturing for a two-layer closure.

The surgeon begins closing the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) defect, a critical step to prevent internal hernias. The newly formed space beneath the anastomosis is visible, where small bowel could potentially become kinked and lead to strangulation.

The surgeon sutures the Roux limb to the bottom of the stomach while adjusting the patient's position from flat to reverse Trendelenburg. This positioning facilitates the creation of the gastric pouch and provides easy access for the upcoming gastrojejunal anastomosis.

The surgeon begins creating the gastric pouch by entering through the pars flaccida. The transection point is identified while preserving the left gastric artery. Using a vessel sealer, the lesser sac vessels are divided at the chosen transection point on the stomach, approximately five to six centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to perform horizontal stapling on the gastric pouch, ensuring the stomach is clear before using a 60mm stapler load. After horizontal stapling, a 36 French Visigy tube is placed from the esophagus into the gastric pouch. The surgeon then introduces the robotic stapler for vertical stapling, snugging it against the Visigy tube to aid in pouch creation.

The surgeon positions the Roux limb in opposition to the newly created gastric pouch. A stay stitch is placed at the apex of the anastomosis to retract the Roux limb and stomach. Using scissors, the surgeon creates a gastrotomy against the Visigy, followed by an aneurotomy.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to create the gastrojejunal anastomosis, inserting approximately thirty millimeters of the sixty millimeter stapler. A bougie is passed through the anastomosis to ensure patency and provide a guide for suturing. The surgeon employs a V-lock suture technique similar to that used in the closure of the jejunostomy anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a two-layer closure of the gastrojejunal anastomosis, ensuring the bottom corner is completely closed to prevent leakage. As the suturing reaches the apex towards the top corner of the common channel, lumbar suturing is initiated. Large suture bites are taken to avoid narrowing the anastomosis, with a bougie in place to maintain patency.

The surgeon applies a VLOC suture to relieve tension at the apex of the gastrojejunal anastomosis, reinforcing the stay suture. Lumbar sutures are placed across the anterior surface of the anastomosis, ensuring reinforcement without concern for narrowing due to the presence of a bougie.

The surgeon inserts a scope through the mouth, down the esophagus, into the gastric pouch, and through the gastrojejunal anastomosis. The assistant dribbles water over the anastomosis while air is blown through it, checking for bubbling to ensure patency. No bubbling is observed, confirming the anastomosis is widely patent and the bypass is successful.
